nativeRange.habitat: Mystery Snails are native to slow-moving freshwaters with dense vegetation, including swamps, ponds, marshes, and the calmer margins of streams across South America. They are typically reported from hard, mineral-rich water. In the wild they are active primarily at night, crawling over plants and substrate to graze on biofilm and organic debris.

decorPreferences: tightly-fitting lid (climbs out of open tanks)
3–5 cm humid air gap between water surface and lid for above-water egg deposition
live or silk plants and driftwood for grazing surfaces
smooth rockwork and gravel or sand — no sharp décor that can chip the shell

diet.notes: The Mystery Snail is generally considered an enthusiastic scavenger and grazer. It has been documented eating soft film algae, biofilm, leftover fish food, blanched vegetables such as spinach, kale, and zucchini, sinking pellets, dead plant matter, and deceased tankmates. In soft or low-mineral water, calcium-rich supplements — such as cuttlebone, calcium-fortified pellets, or gel foods with added calcium — are generally advised to support healthy shell growth.

avoidWith: loaches that eat snails (clown loach, yoyo loach)

summary: The Mystery Snail is a peaceful, plant-safe apple snail from South America, reaching about 4–5 cm across the shell. Widely kept in planted freshwater aquaria, it is available in several colour morphs — gold, blue, ivory, magenta, and jade — that share identical care requirements. Unlike the invasive Pomacea canaliculata, it does not damage healthy live plants.
careNotes: Mystery Snails are generally considered one of the most beginner-friendly freshwater snails. They are air-breathers that surface periodically using a long siphon, so a humid air gap of roughly 3–5 cm between the water surface and a tight-fitting lid is advised — both to let them breathe and to allow the above-water egg deposition described below. The lid should be tightly fitted in all directions; they are well-documented climbers and can leave open or loosely-covered tanks via heater cords, filter intakes or air-line tubing. Hard, alkaline water with plenty of calcium is generally advised to support shell growth; pitted or eroded shells are commonly read as a sign of soft water or calcium deficiency. Like other aquatic invertebrates they are highly sensitive to copper, so copper-based medications, treatments or fertilisers containing significant copper should be removed or substituted while the snail is in the tank. Salt treatments should also generally be avoided. Important taxonomy note: the name 'Apple Snail' is sometimes used broadly in the trade and can refer to the much larger, plant-destroying and widely invasive Pomacea canaliculata, which is banned or restricted in many jurisdictions — that species is tracked as a separate entry (fw-moll-003) and should not be confused with the small, non-destructive P. bridgesii kept in home aquaria. Pomacea diffusa is treated by recent literature as a closely related species (or subspecies) with effectively identical aquarium care, and the bulk of pet-trade 'Mystery Snails' are likely some mix of the two. Colour morphs (gold, blue, ivory, magenta, jade and others) are shell-pigment variants only and share identical care requirements.
breedingNotes: Generally considered easy to breed if a male and a female are present. Sexing is difficult at purchase. Eggs are laid ABOVE the water line in a large, pink-to-orange clutch resembling an elongated blackberry, so a humid air gap between the water surface and the lid is required for successful clutches. Clutch sizes range from roughly 50–100 eggs in young females to several hundred in mature animals; eggs hatch in roughly 1–4 weeks depending on temperature, and hatchlings drop into the water as miniature adults. To prevent breeding, keep only one snail, start with juveniles, fill the tank close to the top, or remove clutches before hatching.

additional.name: The Shrimp Farm
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 on access (both the homepage and the mystery-snail care article); could not be cross-checked. Listed for traceability; swapped to Aquariadise as substitute secondary.

additional.name: Aquariadise
additional.notes: Fallback secondary used in place of blocked Shrimp Farm. Confirmed family Ampullariidae, adult shell size around 5 cm, hard-water preference, copper sensitivity, climbing/escape behaviour, native range (Brazil/Paraguay/Peru/Bolivia), and overlapping temperature/pH ranges (20–28.9°C, pH 7.6–8.4, kH 12–18).
